trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral to skeptical at the credit-qualifier exchange and never fully recovers. Joel completes a partial verify, adapts reasonably when bill history is unavailable, but exits without any button-up elements — no locked time, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The outcome is an unscheduled callback promise, which is structurally fragile.
outcome_dispute: No v1 tag was provided. The observable outcome is a callback promised but unscheduled. If a v1 tag of 'booked' or 'appointment_set' exists in the system, it should be reclassified — zero button_up checklist items were completed, no time was locked, and the closer has no confirmed slot to work from.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up execution — locking the appointment before hanging up
primary_coaching_focus.quote: so once that the design is finished here, I'll give you another call here. We can find a good time for our specialist to give you a call and just kinda review that with you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Joel adapted well when bill history was unavailable and kept the call moving, but he exited without a single button_up element in place. The result is a callback intention with no locked time, no spouse included, no show-rate anchors. For a remote-property, absentee-owner lead with an unresolved credibility dent, this is the highest-risk close pattern on the floor. The coaching conversation should be concrete: walk through the button_up checklist, role-play the close from 06:14, and establish that 'design not ready' is not a reason to skip locking the specialist call time.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. But I'm just curious. What kinda got you interested in solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: Joel asks the right question but the delivery is buried mid-task ('let me get that pulled up here on Google Maps real quick... yeah but I'm just curious'). The question lands as a checkbox, not a genuine open. Mike's answer — power outages, backup — is a high-value emotional anchor and Joel acknowledges it in one line before moving on. The schema calls this the emotional anchor and notes top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Joel spends roughly 8 seconds.

key_moments.quote: The power shuts off out there periodically.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mike's primary motivation is power reliability, not bill savings. This is a battery-backup lead, not a cost-reduction lead. Joel acknowledges it ('So you're kinda looking into the backup options?') and Mike confirms ('Yeah. Mainly. Yeah.') — and then Joel never returns to it. The entire bill-collection sequence that follows is oriented around cost-based design sizing, which is the wrong frame for this customer's stated need. The closer will walk in cold on the actual motivation.

key_moments.quote: So be straight with me because I do understand what the system and how it works.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mike correctly identifies that the federal tax credit does not require a minimum credit score — that's Ion's program requirement, not the IRS's. Joel's script conflates the two under 'incentives,' and when Mike calls it out, Joel's response ('Yeah. No. That's qualify me. Okay?') is fragmented and doesn't cleanly separate the two. Joel then repeats 'incentives' language at 03:41, re-triggering the same confusion. This is a knowledge gap, not a strategic deferral — Joel doesn't have a clean answer for why Ion requires a 650 credit score, and the fumble costs him credibility with an informed buyer.

key_moments.quote: I have two lots up there. I have the one that has the cabin on it, which is the one in question, and then I have a fifth wheel that's sitting right beside it in a 40 foot camp, carport.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mike volunteers that he has two structures on the property — a cabin and a fifth wheel with a carport — and that he lives in Texas, making this a secondary/remote property. Joel acknowledges the geography ('Okay. Gotcha.') but doesn't probe the second structure or the remote-ownership context. The lead profile is materially richer than a standard residential solar inquiry: two structures, absentee owner, backup-power motivation. None of this context is captured for the closer. Note: cross-sell routing for secondary structures is not yet an active schema directive (the schema marks this TBD under cross_sell_signals), so this is flagged as a lead-profile enrichment miss rather than a routing failure.
